event: Rebecca at the Well — Eliezer Meets Rebecca
symbols: the well at the center of the composition — the meeting place of divine providence, wells in Scripture being sites of betrothal and covenantal encounter (Genesis 24:11, 29:2, Exodus 2:15)
the woman offering water — Rebecca's act of extraordinary hospitality, the sign Eliezer prayed for to identify Isaac's bride (Genesis 24:14)
the sheep and livestock — the pastoral wealth of the patriarchal families, and the flocks that Rebecca offers to water
the expansive landscape with distant town — the land of Aram-Naharaim (Mesopotamia), the ancestral homeland from which Abraham's family came
the richly dressed male figure — Eliezer, Abraham's trusted servant, sent with the solemn charge to find a bride for Isaac among Abraham's kin
the women gathered around the well — the daughters of the townsmen who came to draw water at evening (Genesis 24:13)
description: A large Baroque landscape painting shown within its ornate gilt frame. The scene depicts a group of figures gathered around a stone well in a pastoral setting. At the left, several richly dressed figures — a man in red robes and women in elegant attire — converse near the well. At center, a young woman in lighter garments stands gracefully, appearing to offer or draw water. To the right, figures tend to sheep and livestock near the well, with one figure reclining and children playing with lambs.
theological_description: This scene depicts the pivotal encounter at the well in Genesis 24, when Abraham's servant Eliezer travels to Aram-Naharaim to find a wife for Isaac. Eliezer prays for a specific sign: the woman who offers water to him and his camels will be the one God has appointed (Genesis 24:12-14). Rebecca's immediate and generous response — 'Drink, my lord' and 'I will draw water for your camels also' (Genesis 24:18-19) — confirms her as God's chosen bride for Isaac. This narrative is one of the great providential stories of the Old Testament, demonstrating God's faithful guidance of the patriarchal covenant. The well as a betrothal scene is a recurring biblical type-scene: Jacob meets Rachel at a well (Genesis 29:1-12), Moses meets Zipporah at a well (Exodus 2:15-21), and Jesus encounters the Samaritan woman at Jacob's well (John 4:1-42). In patristic and medieval typology, Rebecca prefigures the Church as the Bride of Christ — she who responds with faith and generosity to the call that comes from afar. Eliezer's role as intermediary foreshadows the Holy Spirit who draws the Bride to the Bridegroom. The water she draws from the well becomes a symbol of grace freely offered, anticipating Christ's promise of living water (John 4:10-14). Rebecca's willing departure from her homeland to join Isaac (Genesis 24:58) mirrors the faith of Abraham himself, who left his country at God's command (Genesis 12:1).

scripture_refs.reason: Eliezer's journey to find a bride for Isaac and his encounter with Rebecca at the well — the scene directly depicted
Eliezer's recounting of his prayer and the sign God provided through Rebecca's hospitality
Rebecca's willing consent to leave her family and become Isaac's wife, completing the betrothal narrative
Jacob meeting Rachel at a well — the recurring type-scene of betrothal at wells in patriarchal narrative
Jesus offering living water at Jacob's well — the christological fulfillment of the well as a site of covenantal encounter
Abraham's call to leave his country — echoed in Rebecca's faith to depart her homeland for an unknown bridegroom
